Interactive unit converter¶

Type a value, pick its unit, and the equivalent in every other unit updates instantly. The cell runs live in your browser, so no installation is needed.

From classical to Quantum¶

Blackbody radiation¶

DescriptionEquations
Energy quantizationE=nhνE = nh\nuE=nhν
Average energy of an oscillating dipole⟨Eosc⟩=hνehν/kBT−1\langle E_{\text{osc}} \rangle = \dfrac{h\nu}{e^{h\nu / k_B T} - 1}⟨Eosc​⟩=ehν/kB​T−1hν​
Spectral radiation density of blackbody (Planck)ρ(ν,T) dν=8πhν3c31ehν/kBT−1 dν\rho (\nu, T) \ d\nu = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3}\dfrac{1}{e^{h\nu / k_B T} - 1} \ d\nuρ(ν,T) dν=c38πhν3​ehν/kB​T−11​ dν
Spectral radiation density of blackbody (classical, Rayleigh-Jeans)ρ(ν,T) dν=8πν2c3kBT dν\rho (\nu, T) \ d\nu = \dfrac{8\pi \nu^2}{c^3} k_B T \ d\nuρ(ν,T) dν=c38πν2​kB​T dν

DescriptionEquations
Energy of lightE=hνE = h \nuE=hν
Photoelectric effect
Kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron		Ek=hν−ΦE_k = h \nu - \PhiEk​=hν−Φ
de Broglie relationp=hλp = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}p=λh​
Kinetic energyEk=12mv2=p22mE_k = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = \dfrac{p^2}{2m}Ek​=21​mv2=2mp2​

DescriptionEquations
Hydrogen emission lines
n2>n1n_2 > n_1n2​>n1​		ν~=1λ=RH(1n12−1n22)\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)ν~=λ1​=RH​(n12​1​−n22​1​)
Bohr’s radiusr=4πε0ℏ2mee2r = \dfrac{4\pi \varepsilon_0 \hbar^2}{m_e e^2}r=me​e24πε0​ℏ2​
Energy level in Bohr’s modelEn=−mee48ε02h2n2E_n = -\dfrac{m_e e^4}{8\varepsilon_0^2 h^2n^2}En​=−8ε02​h2n2me​e4​
Emission of hydrogen atom
n2>n1n_2 > n_1n2​>n1​		ν=mee48ε02h3(1n12−1n22)\nu = \dfrac{m_e e^4}{8\varepsilon_0^2 h^3} \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)ν=8ε02​h3me​e4​(n12​1​−n22​1​)

Waves¶

DescriptionEquations
Classical nondispersive wave equation∂2Ψ(x,t)∂x2=1v2∂2Ψ(x,t)∂t2\dfrac{\partial^2 \Psi(x, t)}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{1}{v^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 \Psi(x, t)}{\partial t^2}∂x2∂2Ψ(x,t)​=v21​∂t2∂2Ψ(x,t)​
Wave numberk=2πλk = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}k=λ2π​
Frequencyν=1T\nu = \dfrac{1}{T}ν=T1​
Angular frequencyω=2πT=2πν\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T} = 2\pi\nuω=T2π​=2πν
Wave speedv=λνv = \lambda\nuv=λν
Euler’s formulaeiθ=cos⁡θ+isin⁡θe^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\thetaeiθ=cosθ+isinθ
Solution of wave equationΨ(x,t)=Asin⁡(kx−ωt+ϕ) =Re(Aei(kx−ωt+ϕ′))\begin{aligned}\Psi(x, t) &= A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi) \\\ &= \mathrm{Re}(Ae^{i(kx-\omega t + \phi')})\end{aligned}Ψ(x,t) ​=Asin(kx−ωt+ϕ)=Re(Aei(kx−ωt+ϕ′))​
Interfering traveling waves give standing waveΨ(x,t)=A[sin⁡(kx−ωt)+sin⁡(kx+ωt)] =2Asin⁡(kx)cos⁡(ωt) =ψ(x)cos⁡(ωt)\begin{aligned}\Psi(x, t) &= A[\sin(kx - \omega t) + \sin(kx + \omega t)] \\\ &= 2A \sin(kx)\cos(\omega t) \\\ &= \psi(x)\cos(\omega t) \end{aligned}Ψ(x,t)  ​=A[sin(kx−ωt)+sin(kx+ωt)]=2Asin(kx)cos(ωt)=ψ(x)cos(ωt)​
Time-independent Schrodinger equation−ℏ22md2ψ(x)dx2+V(x)ψ(x)=Eψ(x)-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\dfrac{d^2\psi(x)}{dx^2} + V(x)\psi(x) = E\psi(x)−2mℏ2​dx2d2ψ(x)​+V(x)ψ(x)=Eψ(x)
Time-dependent Schrodinger equation−ℏ22m∂2Ψ(x,t)∂x2+V(x,t)Ψ(x,t)=iℏ∂Ψ(x,t)∂t-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\dfrac{\partial^2\Psi(x, t)}{\partial x^2} + V(x, t)\Psi(x, t) = i\hbar\dfrac{\partial \Psi(x, t)}{\partial t}−2mℏ2​∂x2∂2Ψ(x,t)​+V(x,t)Ψ(x,t)=iℏ∂t∂Ψ(x,t)​
Stationary states are standing wavesΨ(x,t)=ψ(x)e−i(E/ℏ)t\Psi(x, t) = \psi(x) e^{-i(E/\hbar)t}Ψ(x,t)=ψ(x)e−i(E/ℏ)t
Normalization∥f(x)∥=∫Df∗f dx=1\Vert f(x) \Vert = \int_D f^* f \ dx = 1∥f(x)∥=∫D​f∗f dx=1
Orthogonality∫Df∗g dx=0\int_D f^* g \ dx = 0∫D​f∗g dx=0
Use quantum mechanics when ...1. λparticle∼Lproblem\lambda_{\text{particle}} \sim L_{\text{problem}}λparticle​∼Lproblem​
2. ΔE≳kbT\Delta E \gtrsim k_bTΔE≳kb​T (discrete energy spectrum)

Quantum-Mechanical Postulates¶

  1. The state of a quantum-mechanical particle is completely specified by a wave function Ψ(x,t)\Psi(x, t)Ψ(x,t). The probability that the particle will be found at time t0t_0t0​ in a spatial interval of width dxdxdx centered at x0x_0x0​ is given by Ψ∗(x0,t0)Ψ(x0,t0)dx\Psi^*(x_0, t_0)\Psi(x_0, t_0) dxΨ∗(x0​,t0​)Ψ(x0​,t0​)dx

  2. For every measurable property of a system, there exists a corresponding operator.

  3. In any single measurement of the observable that corresponds to the operator A^\hat{A}A^, the only values that will ever be measured are the eigenvalues of that operator.

  4. If the system is in a state described by the wave function Ψ(x,t)\Psi(x, t)Ψ(x,t), and the value of the observatle aaa is measured once on each of many identically prepared systems, the average value (expectation value) of all of the measurements is given by

    ⟨a⟩=∫−∞∞Ψ∗A^Ψ dx∫−∞∞Ψ∗Ψ dx\langle a \rangle = \dfrac{\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Psi^* \hat{A} \Psi \ dx}{\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Psi^*\Psi \ dx}⟨a⟩=∫−∞∞​Ψ∗Ψ dx∫−∞∞​Ψ∗A^Ψ dx​
    (1)

  5. The evolution in time of a quantum-mechanical system is governed by the time-dependent Schrödinger equation

    H^Ψ(x,t)=iℏ∂Ψ(x,t)∂t\hat{H}\Psi(x, t) = i\hbar\dfrac{\partial\Psi(x, t)}{\partial t}H^Ψ(x,t)=iℏ∂t∂Ψ(x,t)​
    (2)

Operators¶

Description1D3D
Positionx^=x\hat{x} = xx^=xx^=x\mathbf{\hat{x}} = \mathbf{x}x^=x
Linear momentump^x=−iℏddx\hat{p}_x = -i\hbar \dfrac{d}{dx}p^​x​=−iℏdxd​p^=−iℏ∇\mathbf{\hat{p}} = -i\hbar\mathbf{\nabla}p^​=−iℏ∇
Kinetic energyT^=p^x22m=−ℏ22md2dx2\hat{T} = \dfrac{\hat{p}_x^2}{2m} = -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\dfrac{d^2}{dx^2}T^=2mp^​x2​​=−2mℏ2​dx2d2​T^=−ℏ22m∇2\mathbf{\hat{T}} = -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\mathbf{\nabla}^2T^=−2mℏ2​∇2
Potential energyV^=V(x)\hat{V} = V(x)V^=V(x)V^=V(x)\mathbf{\hat{V}} = V(\mathbf{x})V^=V(x)
Total energy HamiltonianH^=T^+V^=−ℏ22md2dx2+V(x)\hat{H} = \hat{T} + \hat{V} = -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\dfrac{d^2}{dx^2} + V(x)H^=T^+V^=−2mℏ2​dx2d2​+V(x)H^=−ℏ22m∇2+V(x)\mathbf{\hat{H}} = -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\mathbf{\nabla}^2 + V(\mathbf{x})H^=−2mℏ2​∇2+V(x)

Simple Quantum Systems¶

Stationary states¶

DescriptionEquations
Time dependent Schrodinger equationH^Ψ(x,t)=iℏ∂Ψ(x,t)∂t\hat{H}\Psi(x, t) = i\hbar\dfrac{\partial\Psi(x, t)}{\partial t}H^Ψ(x,t)=iℏ∂t∂Ψ(x,t)​
Time independent Schrodinger equationH^ψn(x)=Enψn(x)\hat{H}\psi_n(x) = E_n \psi_n(x)H^ψn​(x)=En​ψn​(x)
Stationary state wave functionΨ(x,t)=ψ(x)T(t)\Psi(x, t) = \psi(x) T(t)Ψ(x,t)=ψ(x)T(t)
Time component of wave functionT(t)=e−iEt/ℏT(t) = e^{-iEt/\hbar}T(t)=e−iEt/ℏ
Probability of finding particle in an intervalProb(x,x+dx)=∣Ψ(x,t)∣2dx=∣ψ(x)∣2dx\mathrm{Prob}(x, x+dx) = \vert \Psi(x, t) \vert^2 dx = \vert \psi(x) \vert^2 dxProb(x,x+dx)=∣Ψ(x,t)∣2dx=∣ψ(x)∣2dx
General solution as linear combination of stationary statesψ(x)=∑ncnϕn(x)\psi(x) = \sum\limits_n c_n \phi_n(x)ψ(x)=n∑​cn​ϕn​(x)
Expansion coefficientscn=⟨ϕn∣ψ⟩=∫ϕn∗ψ dxc_n = \langle \phi_n \vert \psi \rangle = \int \phi_n^* \psi \ dxcn​=⟨ϕn​∣ψ⟩=∫ϕn∗​ψ dx
Normalization$\sum\limits_n

Particle in a 1D box¶

DescriptionEquations
Time independent Schrodinger equation[−ℏ22md2dx2+V(x)]ψ(x)=Eψ(x)\left[ -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\dfrac{d^2}{dx^2} + V(x) \right] \psi(x) = E\psi(x)[−2mℏ2​dx2d2​+V(x)]ψ(x)=Eψ(x)
Wave function
n=1,2,3,...n = 1, 2, 3, ...n=1,2,3,...		ψn(x)=2Lsin⁡(nπxL)\psi_n(x) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{L}} \sin\left(\dfrac{n \pi x}{L}\right)ψn​(x)=L2​​sin(Lnπx​)
Energy eigenvaluesEn=h28mL2n2=ℏ2π22mL2n2E_n = \dfrac{h^2}{8mL^2} n^2 = \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2mL^2}n^2En​=8mL2h2​n2=2mL2ℏ2π2​n2

Particle in a 3D box¶

DescriptionEquations
Time independent Schrodinger equation[−ℏ22m∇2+V(x)]ψ(x)=Eψ(x)\left[ -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\mathbf{\nabla}^2 + V(\mathbf{x}) \right] \psi(\mathbf{x}) = E\psi(\mathbf{x})[−2mℏ2​∇2+V(x)]ψ(x)=Eψ(x)
Wave function
nx=1,2,3,...n_x = 1, 2, 3, ...nx​=1,2,3,...
ny=1,2,3,...n_y = 1, 2, 3, ...ny​=1,2,3,...
nz=1,2,3,...n_z = 1, 2, 3, ...nz​=1,2,3,...		ψnx,ny,nz(x) =ψnx(x)ψny(y)ψnz(z) =2Lx2Ly2Lzsin⁡(nxπxLx)sin⁡(nyπyLy)sin⁡(nzπzLz)\begin{aligned}&\psi_{n_x, n_y, n_z}(\mathbf{x}) \\\ =& \psi_{n_x}(x)\psi_{n_y}(y)\psi_{n_z}(z) \\\ =& \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{L_x}}\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{L_y}}\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{L_z}} \sin\left(\dfrac{n_x \pi x}{L_x}\right)\sin\left(\dfrac{n_y \pi y}{L_y}\right)\sin\left(\dfrac{n_z \pi z}{L_z}\right)\end{aligned} = =​ψnx​,ny​,nz​​(x)ψnx​​(x)ψny​​(y)ψnz​​(z)Lx​2​​Ly​2​​Lz​2​​sin(Lx​nx​πx​)sin(Ly​ny​πy​)sin(Lz​nz​πz​)​
Energy eigenvaluesEn=h28m(nx2Lx2+ny2Ly2+nz2Lz2)E_n = \dfrac{h^2}{8m} \left(\dfrac{n_x^2}{L_x^2} + \dfrac{n_y^2}{L_y^2} + \dfrac{n_z^2}{L_z^2}\right)En​=8mh2​(Lx2​nx2​​+Ly2​ny2​​+Lz2​nz2​​)

Finite potential well¶

DescriptionEquations
PotentialV(x)={0x∈[0,L] V0elsewhereV(x) = \begin{cases}0 & x\in [0, L] \\\ V_0 & \mathrm{elsewhere}\end{cases}V(x)={0 V0​​x∈[0,L]elsewhere​
Reflection probabilityR=(E−E−V0)2(E+E−V0)2R = \dfrac{(\sqrt{E} - \sqrt{E - V_0})^2}{(\sqrt{E} + \sqrt{E - V_0})^2}R=(E​+E−V0​​)2(E​−E−V0​​)2​
Transmission probabilityT=4E(E−V0)(E+E−V0)2T = \dfrac{4\sqrt{E(E - V_0)}}{(\sqrt{E} + \sqrt{E - V_0})^2}T=(E​+E−V0​​)24E(E−V0​)​​

Commutators and Uncertainty¶

DescriptionEquations
Commutator[A,B]=AB−BA[A, B] = AB - BA[A,B]=AB−BA
Condition of commutation[A,B]=0[A, B] = 0[A,B]=0
Standard deviation (uncertainty)σA=⟨(A−⟨A⟩)2⟩ =⟨A2⟩−⟨A⟩2\begin{aligned}\sigma_A &= \sqrt{\langle (A - \langle A \rangle)^2 \rangle} \\\ &= \sqrt{\langle A^2 \rangle - \langle A \rangle^2}\end{aligned}σA​ ​=⟨(A−⟨A⟩)2⟩​=⟨A2⟩−⟨A⟩2​​
Heisenberg uncertainty principle (general)σAσB≥12∣⟨[A^,B^]⟩∣\sigma_A \sigma_B \ge \frac{1}{2} \vert\langle[\hat{A}, \hat{B}]\rangle\vertσA​σB​≥21​∣⟨[A^,B^]⟩∣
Heisenberg uncertainty principle (position-momentum)σxσp≥ℏ2\sigma_x \sigma_p \ge \frac{\hbar}{2}σx​σp​≥2ℏ​

Spectroscopy¶

Vibration: quantum harmonic oscillator¶

DescriptionEquations
Vibrational Schrodinger equation[−ℏ22μ1r2∂∂r(r2∂∂r)+V(r)]ψ(x)=Eψ(x)\left[ -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2\mu}\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\right) + V(r) \right]\psi(\mathbf{x}) = E\psi(\mathbf{x})[−2μℏ2​r21​∂r∂​(r2∂r∂​)+V(r)]ψ(x)=Eψ(x)
Wave functionψ(x)=R(r)Y(θ,ϕ)\psi(\mathbf{x}) = R(r)Y(\theta, \phi)ψ(x)=R(r)Y(θ,ϕ)
Harmonic approximationV(r)≈12kr2V(r) \approx \frac{1}{2}kr^2V(r)≈21​kr2
Spring constantk=μω02k = \mu\omega_0^2k=μω02​
Vibrational Schrodinger equation[−ℏ22μ1r2∂∂r(r2∂∂r)+12kr2]ψ(r)=Eψ(r)\left[ -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2\mu}\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{2}kr^2 \right]\psi(r) = E\psi(r)[−2μℏ2​r21​∂r∂​(r2∂r∂​)+21​kr2]ψ(r)=Eψ(r)
Wave function
n=0,1,2,...n = 0, 1, 2, ...n=0,1,2,...		ψ(r)=12nn!(απ)1/4Hn(αr)e−αr2/2\psi(r) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2^n n!}}\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{\pi}\right)^{1/4} H_n(\sqrt{\alpha} r) e^{-\alpha r^2 / 2}ψ(r)=2nn!​1​(πα​)1/4Hn​(α​r)e−αr2/2
Hermite polynomialsHn(x)=(−1)nex2(dndxn)e−x2H_n(x) = (-1)^n e^{x^2}\left(\dfrac{d^n}{dx^n}\right)e^{-x^2}Hn​(x)=(−1)nex2(dxndn​)e−x2
Constantα=mω0ℏ\alpha = \dfrac{m\omega_0}{\hbar}α=ℏmω0​​
Energy eigenvalue
n=0,1,2,...n = 0, 1, 2, ...n=0,1,2,...		En=(n+12)ℏω0E_n = (n + \frac{1}{2})\hbar \omega_0En​=(n+21​)ℏω0​
Transition dipole momentμ⃗fi=dμ⃗(x0)dx⟨ψf∣x^∣ψi⟩\vec{\mu}_{fi} = \dfrac{d\vec{\mu}(x_0)}{dx} \langle \psi_f \vert \hat{x} \vert \psi_i \rangleμ​fi​=dxdμ​(x0​)​⟨ψf​∣x^∣ψi​⟩
Vibrational selection ruleΔn=±1\Delta n = \pm 1Δn=±1

Rotation: quantum rigid rotor¶

Classical rigid rotor¶

DescriptionEquations
Angular momentumL=x×p=Iω⃗\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{x} \times \mathbf{p} = I\vec{\omega}L=x×p=Iω
Linear velocityv⃗=R0ω⃗\vec{v} = R_0 \vec{\omega}v=R0​ω
Moment of inertiaI=mR02I = mR_0^2I=mR02​
Rotational kinetic energyE=12Iω2=L22IE = \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \dfrac{L^2}{2I}E=21​Iω2=2IL2​

Quantum rigid rotor¶

DescriptionEquations
Angular momentum operatorL^=x^×p^\hat{\mathbf{L}} = \hat{\mathbf{x}} \times \hat{\mathbf{p}}L^=x^×p^​
z-component of angular momentum operatorL^z=ℏi∂∂ϕ\hat{L}_z = \dfrac{\hbar}{i}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial\phi}L^z​=iℏ​∂ϕ∂​
Magnitude of angular momentum operatorL^2=L2=−ℏ2[1sin⁡θ∂∂θ(sin⁡θ∂∂θ)+1sin⁡2θ∂2∂ϕ2]\hat{\mathbf{L}}^2 = L^2 = -\hbar^2 \left[ \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial\theta} \left( \sin\theta\dfrac{\partial}{\partial\theta} \right) + \dfrac{1}{\sin^2\theta}\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial\phi^2} \right]L^2=L2=−ℏ2[sinθ1​∂θ∂​(sinθ∂θ∂​)+sin2θ1​∂ϕ2∂2​]
Components of L^\hat{\mathbf{L}}L^ does not commute[L^i,L^j]=iℏL^k[\hat{L}_i, \hat{L}_j] = i\hbar \hat{L}_k[L^i​,L^j​]=iℏL^k​
Components of L^\hat{\mathbf{L}}L^ commute with its magnitude[L^i,L2]=0[\hat{L}_i, L^2] = 0[L^i​,L2]=0
DescriptionEquations
Rotational Schrodinger equation−ℏ22μR02[1r2sin⁡θ∂∂θ(sin⁡θ∂∂θ)+1r2sin⁡2θ∂2∂ϕ2]Y(θ,ϕ)=EY(θ,ϕ)-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2\mu R_0^2}\left[ \dfrac{1}{r^2 \sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\sin\theta\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2 \sin^2 \theta}\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial\phi^2} \right]Y(\theta, \phi) = EY(\theta, \phi)−2μR02​ℏ2​[r2sinθ1​∂θ∂​(sinθ∂θ∂​)+r2sin2θ1​∂ϕ2∂2​]Y(θ,ϕ)=EY(θ,ϕ)
Spherical harmonicsYlm(θ,ϕ)=(−)m(2l+1)4π(l−m)!(l+m)!Plm(cos⁡θ)eimϕY_l^m(\theta, \phi) = (-)^m \sqrt{\dfrac{(2l+1)}{4\pi}\dfrac{(l-m)!}{(l+m)!}} P_l^m(\cos\theta) e^{im\phi}Ylm​(θ,ϕ)=(−)m4π(2l+1)​(l+m)!(l−m)!​​Plm​(cosθ)eimϕ
Legendre polynomialPlm(x)=12ll!(1−x2)m/2d(l+m)dx(l+m)(x2−1)lP_l^m(x) = \dfrac{1}{2^l l!}(1-x^2)^{m/2} \dfrac{d^{(l+m)}}{dx^{(l+m)}}(x^2-1)^lPlm​(x)=2ll!1​(1−x2)m/2dx(l+m)d(l+m)​(x2−1)l
Energy eigenvalues
l=0,1,2,...l = 0, 1, 2, ...l=0,1,2,...		El=ℏ22Il(l+1)E_l = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{2I}l(l+1)El​=2Iℏ2​l(l+1)
Angular momentum eigenvalues
l=0,1,2,...l = 0, 1, 2, ...l=0,1,2,...		L2Y=ℏ2l(l+1)YL^2 Y = \hbar^2l(l+1) YL2Y=ℏ2l(l+1)Y
z-component eigenvalues
m=−l,...,0,...,lm = -l, ..., 0, ..., lm=−l,...,0,...,l		LzY=ℏmYL_z Y = \hbar m YLz​Y=ℏmY
Transition dipole momentμfi=⟨ψf∣μzcos⁡θ∣ψi⟩\mu_{fi} = \langle \psi_f \vert \mu_z \cos\theta \vert \psi_i \rangleμfi​=⟨ψf​∣μz​cosθ∣ψi​⟩
Rotational selection ruleΔl=±1,Δm=0\Delta l = \pm 1, \Delta m = 0Δl=±1,Δm=0

Hydrogen atom¶

DescriptionEquations
Hydrogen atom Schrodinger equation[−ℏ22me1r2∂∂r(r2∂∂r)+L⃗22mer2−e2r]ψ(x)=Eψ(x)\left[ -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 \dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{\vec{L}^2}{2m_er^2} - \dfrac{e^2}{r} \right]\psi(x) = E\psi(x)[−2me​ℏ2​r21​∂r∂​(r2∂r∂​)+2me​r2L2​−re2​]ψ(x)=Eψ(x)
Effective potentialVeff=ℏ2l(l+1)2mr2−e2rV_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\hbar^2 l(l+1)}{2mr^2} - \dfrac{e^2}{r}Veff​=2mr2ℏ2l(l+1)​−re2​
Wave function
n=1,2,...n = 1, 2, ...n=1,2,...		ψnlm(x)=Rnl(r)Ylm(θ,ϕ)\psi_{nlm}(x) = R_{nl}(r)Y_l^m(\theta, \phi)ψnlm​(x)=Rnl​(r)Ylm​(θ,ϕ)
Energy eigenvalues
n=1,2,...n = 1, 2, ...n=1,2,...		En=−e22a01n2=−me42ℏ21n2=−RHn2E_n = -\dfrac{e^2}{2a_0}\dfrac{1}{n^2} = -\dfrac{me^4}{2\hbar^2}\dfrac{1}{n^2} = -\dfrac{R_H}{n^2}En​=−2a0​e2​n21​=−2ℏ2me4​n21​=−n2RH​​
Rydberg’s constantRH=2.179×10−18J=13.6 eVR_H = 2.179 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J} = 13.6 \ \mathrm{eV}RH​=2.179×10−18J=13.6 eV
Bohr’s radiusa0=ℏ2me2a_0 = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{me^2}a0​=me2ℏ2​
Radial probability distributionPnl(r)dr=r2Rnl2(r)drP_{nl}(r) dr = r^2 R^2_{nl}(r) drPnl​(r)dr=r2Rnl2​(r)dr

Many Electron and Proton System¶

Many electron atom¶

DescriptionEquations
Helium Schrodinger equation[−ℏ22m∇12⏟_KE of e1−−ℏ22m∇22⏞KE of e2−ˆ−2e2∣x1∣⏟_e1−-N attraction−2e2∣x2∣⏞e2−-N attractionˆ+e2∣x1−x2∣⏟_e1−-e2−repulsion]ψ(x1,x2)=Eψ(x1,x2)\left[ \underbrace{-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla_1^2}\_{\text{KE of }e^-_1} \overbrace{-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla_2^2}\^{\text{KE of }e^-_2} \underbrace{-\dfrac{2e^2}{\vert\mathbf{x}_1\vert}}\_{e^-_1\text{-N attraction}} \overbrace{-\dfrac{2e^2}{\vert\mathbf{x}_2\vert}}\^{e^-_2\text{-N attraction}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{e^2}{\vert\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2\vert}}\_{e^-_1 \text{-} e^-_2 \text{repulsion}} \right] \psi(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2) = E \psi(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2)⎣⎡​−2mℏ2​∇12​​_KE of e1−​−2mℏ2​∇22​​KE of e2−​ˆ​−∣x1​∣2e2​​_e1−​-N attraction−∣x2​∣2e2​​e2−​-N attractionˆ​+∣x1​−x2​∣e2​​_e1−​-e2−​repulsion⎦⎤​ψ(x1​,x2​)=Eψ(x1​,x2​)
Orbital approximationψ(x1,x2,…,xn)=ϕ(x1)ϕ(x2)…ϕ(xn)\psi(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_1)\phi(\mathbf{x}_2)\dots\phi(\mathbf{x}_n)ψ(x1​,x2​,…,xn​)=ϕ(x1​)ϕ(x2​)…ϕ(xn​)
Hartree orbital equations[−ℏ2∇i22m−Ze2∣x∣+∑j=1,j≠iN∫e2ϕj∗(x′)ϕj(x′)∣x−x′∣d3x′]ϕi(x)=εiiϕi(x)\left[ -\dfrac{\hbar^2 \nabla_i^2}{2m} - \dfrac{Ze^2}{\vert\mathbf{x}\vert} + \sum\limits^N_{j=1, j\not= i} \displaystyle\int \dfrac{e^2 \phi_j^*(\mathbf{x}')\phi_j(\mathbf{x}')}{\vert\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}' \vert} d^3\mathbf{x}' \right] \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \varepsilon_{ii} \phi_i(\mathbf{x})[−2mℏ2∇i2​​−∣x∣Ze2​+j=1,j=i∑N​∫∣x−x′∣e2ϕj∗​(x′)ϕj​(x′)​d3x′]ϕi​(x)=εii​ϕi​(x)

Spin¶

DescriptionEquations
Components of S^\hat{\mathbf{S}}S^ does not commute[S^i,S^j]=iℏS^k[\hat{S}_i, \hat{S}_j] = i\hbar \hat{S}_k[S^i​,S^j​]=iℏS^k​
Components of S^\hat{\mathbf{S}}S^ commute with its magnitude[S^i,S2]=0[\hat{S}_i, S^2] = 0[S^i​,S2]=0
Eigenvalue of S^2\hat{\mathbf{S}}^2S^2S^2↔ℏ2s(s+1)\hat{\mathbf{S}}^2 \leftrightarrow \hbar^2 s(s+1)S^2↔ℏ2s(s+1)
Eigenvalue of S^z\hat{S}_zS^z​S^z↔ℏms\hat{S}_z \leftrightarrow \hbar m_sS^z​↔ℏms​

Electron spin¶

DescriptionEquations
Electron spins=12s = \frac{1}{2}s=21​
Spin up functionα(ms)={1ms=+12 0ms=−12\alpha(m_s) = \begin{cases} 1 & m_s = +\frac{1}{2} \\\ 0 & m_s = -\frac{1}{2} \end{cases}α(ms​)={1 0​ms​=+21​ms​=−21​​
Spin down functionβ(ms)={0ms=+12 1ms=−12\beta(m_s) = \begin{cases} 0 & m_s = +\frac{1}{2} \\\ 1 & m_s = -\frac{1}{2} \end{cases}β(ms​)={0 1​ms​=+21​ms​=−21​​
α\alphaα is eigenfunction of Sz^\hat{S_z}Sz​^​Sz^α=+12ℏα\hat{S_z} \alpha = +\frac{1}{2}\hbar \alphaSz​^​α=+21​ℏα
β\betaβ is eigenfunction of Sz^\hat{S_z}Sz​^​Sz^β=−12ℏβ\hat{S_z} \beta = -\frac{1}{2}\hbar \betaSz​^​β=−21​ℏβ
α,β\alpha, \betaα,β are eigenfunctions of S2^\hat{S^2}S2^S2^α=ℏ2s(s+1)α=34ℏ2αS2^β=ℏ2s(s+1)β=34ℏ2β\hat{S^2} \alpha = \hbar^2 s(s+1) \alpha = \frac{3}{4} \hbar^2 \alpha \newline \hat{S^2} \beta = \hbar^2 s(s+1) \beta = \frac{3}{4} \hbar^2 \betaS2^α=ℏ2s(s+1)α=43​ℏ2αS2^β=ℏ2s(s+1)β=43​ℏ2β
Normalization∑msα∗α=∑msβ∗β=1\sum\limits_{m_s} \alpha^*\alpha = \sum\limits_{m_s} \beta^*\beta = 1ms​∑​α∗α=ms​∑​β∗β=1
Orthogonality∑msα∗β=∑msβ∗α=0\sum\limits_{m_s} \alpha^*\beta = \sum\limits_{m_s} \beta^*\alpha = 0ms​∑​α∗β=ms​∑​β∗α=0

Identical particles¶

DescriptionEquations
Spin-spin permutation operatorPijψ(r1,r2,…,ri,…,rj,…,rN)=ψ(r1,r2,…,rj,…,ri,…,rN)P_{ij} \psi(\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \dots, \mathbf{r}_i, \dots, \mathbf{r}_j, \dots, \mathbf{r}_N) = \psi(\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \dots, \mathbf{r}_j, \dots, \mathbf{r}_i, \dots, \mathbf{r}_N)Pij​ψ(r1​,r2​,…,ri​,…,rj​,…,rN​)=ψ(r1​,r2​,…,rj​,…,ri​,…,rN​)
Doing nothingPijPij=1P_{ij}P_{ij} = 1Pij​Pij​=1
Symmetric eigenvalueλ=1\lambda = 1λ=1
Anti-symmetric eigenvalueλ=−1\lambda = -1λ=−1
Fermions (e.g. electron)12\frac{1}{2}21​-integer spin, anti-symmetric
Bosonsinteger spin, symmetric
Pauli exclusion principleψ(r1,r2,…,ri,…,ri,…,rN)=−ψ(r1,r2,…,ri,…,ri,…,rN)=0\psi(\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \dots, \mathbf{r}_i, \dots, \mathbf{r}_i, \dots, \mathbf{r}_N) = - \psi(\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \dots, \mathbf{r}_i, \dots, \mathbf{r}_i, \dots, \mathbf{r}_N) = 0ψ(r1​,r2​,…,ri​,…,ri​,…,rN​)=−ψ(r1​,r2​,…,ri​,…,ri​,…,rN​)=0
Slater determinantΨ(x1,x2,⋯ ,xN)=1N!∣χ1(x1)χ2(x1)⋯χN(x1) χ1(x2)χ2(x2)⋯χN(x2) ⋮⋮⋱⋮ χ1(xN)χ2(xN)⋯χN(xN)∣\Psi(\mathrm{x}_1, \mathrm{x}_2, \cdots, \mathrm{x}_N) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N!}} \begin{vmatrix}\chi_1(\mathbf{x}_1) & \chi_2(\mathbf{x}_1) & \cdots & \chi_N(\mathbf{x}_1) \\\ \chi_1(\mathbf{x}_2) & \chi_2(\mathbf{x}_2) & \cdots & \chi_N(\mathbf{x}_2) \\\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\\ \chi_1(\mathbf{x}_N) & \chi_2(\mathbf{x}_N) & \cdots & \chi_N(\mathbf{x}_N) \end{vmatrix}Ψ(x1​,x2​,⋯,xN​)=N!​1​∣∣​χ1​(x1​) χ1​(x2​) ⋮ χ1​(xN​)​χ2​(x1​)χ2​(x2​)⋮χ2​(xN​)​⋯⋯⋱⋯​χN​(x1​)χN​(x2​)⋮χN​(xN​)​∣∣​
Hartree-Fock orbital equations[−ℏ2∇22m−Ze2∣x∣]ϕi(r)+∑j=1N[ϕi(r)∫e2ϕj∗(r)ϕj(r)∣x−x′∣d3r′−ϕj(r)∫e2ϕj∗(r)ϕi(r)∣x−x′∣d3r′]=εiϕi(r)\left[ -\dfrac{\hbar^2\nabla^2}{2m} - \dfrac{Ze^2}{\vert\mathbf{x}\vert} \right] \phi_i(\mathbf{r}) + \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^N \left[ \phi_i(\mathbf{r}) \int \dfrac{e^2 \phi^*_j(\mathbf{r}) \phi_j(\mathbf{r})}{\vert\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}'\vert} d^3r' - \phi_j(\mathbf{r}) \int \dfrac{e^2 \phi^*_j(\mathbf{r}) \phi_i(\mathbf{r})}{\vert\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}'\vert} d^3r' \right] = \varepsilon_i \phi_i(\mathbf{r})[−2mℏ2∇2​−∣x∣Ze2​]ϕi​(r)+j=1∑N​[ϕi​(r)∫∣x−x′∣e2ϕj∗​(r)ϕj​(r)​d3r′−ϕj​(r)∫∣x−x′∣e2ϕj∗​(r)ϕi​(r)​d3r′]=εi​ϕi​(r)
Molecular orbital by linear combination of atomic orbitals (MO-LCAO)ψ(x)=c1ϕ1(x)+c2ϕ2(x)MO=c1(AO)+c2(AO)\psi(\mathbf{x}) = c_1 \phi_1 (\mathbf{x}) + c_2 \phi_2 (\mathbf{x}) \newline \mathrm{MO} = c_1 (\mathrm{AO}) + c_2 (\mathrm{AO})ψ(x)=c1​ϕ1​(x)+c2​ϕ2​(x)MO=c1​(AO)+c2​(AO)
Variational principleE=⟨ψ∣H∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣ψ⟩E = \dfrac{\langle \psi \vert H \vert \psi \rangle}{\langle \psi \vert \psi \rangle}E=⟨ψ∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣H∣ψ⟩​
